steps.reveal: Equo = to the horse (dat. sg., object of 'credite')

hints.ablative: While 'equo' could be ablative, 'credere' takes a dative object — 'equo' is dative here
hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'equus' — 'equo' is dative with 'credite'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'equum' — 'credere' governs the dative, so 'equo' is dative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'equi' ends in -i (2nd declension)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'equi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'equi' ends in -i

hints.to create: That would be 'creare' — 'credite' comes from 'credere' (to trust/believe)
hints.to grow: That would be 'crescere' — 'credite' comes from 'credere' (to trust)
hints.to burn: That would be 'cremare' — 'credite' comes from 'credere' (to trust)

steps.reveal: credite = trust! (imperative pl., with 'ne' = do not trust!)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd pl. would be 'creditis' — 'credite' without -is is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'credatis' — 'credite' is the imperative plural
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'credidistis' — 'credite' is the present imperative

hints.creo, creare, creavi, creatum: These are the parts of 'creare' (to create)
hints.cresco, crescere, crevi, cretum: These are the parts of 'crescere' (to grow)
hints.cerno, cernere, crevi, cretum: These are the parts of 'cernere' (to discern)

hints.nominative: While 'Teucri' looks nominative plural, the imperative 'credite' shows direct address — vocative
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'Teucri' as well, but context demands vocative plural here
hints.dative: Dative would be 'Teucris' — 'Teucri' is vocative plural, matching the imperative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive plural in -arum — 'Teucrorum' ends in -orum (2nd declension)
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive plural in -um/-ium — 'Teucrorum' ends in -orum
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive plural in -uum — 'Teucrorum' ends in -orum

steps.prompt: What is the function of 'ne' in this sentence?

hints.purpose clause marker: 'Ne' can introduce purpose clauses, but here it negates the imperative 'credite'
hints.fear clause marker: 'Ne' appears in fear clauses after 'timeo', but here it simply negates the command
hints.interrogative particle: 'Ne' can be enclitic for questions (-ne), but here it stands alone as a prohibition

translation: Do not trust the horse, Trojans
